data.description:
The board of Enbridge Inc. (TSE:ENB) has announced that it will be paying its dividend of CA$0.97 on the 1st of March, an increased payment from last year's comparable dividend. This makes the dividend yield about the same as the industry average at 5.9%.

Estimates Indicate Enbridge's Could Struggle to Maintain Dividend Payments In The Future

We like a dividend to be consistent over the long term, so checking whether it is sustainable is important. Before making this announcement, the company's dividend was much higher than its earnings. This situation certainly isn't ideal, and could place significant strain on the balance sheet if it continues.

Earnings per share is forecast to rise by 39.2% over the next year. However, if the dividend continues along recent trends, it could start putting pressure on the balance sheet with the payout ratio reaching 114% over the next year.

Enbridge Has A Solid Track Record

The company has a sustained record of paying dividends with very little fluctuation. The annual payment during the last 10 years was CA$1.86 in 2016, and the most recent fiscal year payment was CA$3.88. This means that it has been growing its distributions at 7.6% per annum over that time. Dividends have grown at a reasonable rate over this period, and without any major cuts in the payment over time, we think this is an attractive combination as it provides a nice boost to shareholder returns.

Dividend Growth Could Be Constrained

Investors who have held shares in the company for the past few years will be happy with the dividend income they have received. We are encouraged to see that Enbridge has grown earnings per share at 21% per year over the past five years. While EPS is growing rapidly, Enbridge paid out a very high 145% of its income as dividends. If earnings continue to grow, this dividend may be sustainable, but we think a payout this high definitely bears watching.

Enbridge's Dividend Doesn't Look Sustainable

Overall, this is probably not a great income stock, even though the dividend is being raised at the moment. Although they have been consistent in the past, we think the payments are a little high to be sustained. We don't think Enbridge is a great stock to add to your portfolio if income is your focus.

data.title: Are Hillman Solutions Corp.'s (NASDAQ:HLMN) Fundamentals Good Enough to Warrant Buying Given The Stock's Recent Weakness?
data.description:
It is hard to get excited after looking at Hillman Solutions' (NASDAQ:HLMN) recent performance, when its stock has declined 6.0% over the past week. However, the company's fundamentals look pretty decent, and long-term financials are usually aligned with future market price movements. Specifically, we decided to study Hillman Solutions' ROE in this article.

Return on equity or ROE is a key measure used to assess how efficiently a company's management is utilizing the company's capital. In short, ROE shows the profit each dollar generates with respect to its shareholder investments.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

How Do You Calculate Return On Equity?

Return on equity can be calculated by using the formula:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for Hillman Solutions is:

3.0% = US$37m ÷ US$1.2b (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' is the amount earned after tax over the last twelve months. So, this means that for every $1 of its shareholder's investments, the company generates a profit of $0.03.

View our latest analysis for Hillman Solutions

What Is The Relationship Between ROE And Earnings Growth?

We have already established that ROE serves as an efficient profit-generating gauge for a company's future earnings. We now need to evaluate how much profit the company reinvests or "retains" for future growth which then gives us an idea about the growth potential of the company. Assuming everything else remains unchanged, the higher the ROE and profit retention, the higher the growth rate of a company compared to companies that don't necessarily bear these characteristics.

Hillman Solutions' Earnings Growth And 3.0% ROE

As you can see, Hillman Solutions' ROE looks pretty weak. Not just that, even compared to the industry average of 11%, the company's ROE is entirely unremarkable. In spite of this, Hillman Solutions was able to grow its net income considerably, at a rate of 57% in the last five years. Therefore, there could be other reasons behind this growth. For instance, the company has a low payout ratio or is being managed efficiently.

Next, on comparing with the industry net income growth, we found that Hillman Solutions' growth is quite high when compared to the industry average growth of 16% in the same period, which is great to see.

Earnings growth is a huge factor in stock valuation. The investor should try to establish if the expected growth or decline in earnings, whichever the case may be, is priced in. By doing so, they will have an idea if the stock is headed into clear blue waters or if swampy waters await. Is Hillman Solutions fairly valued compared to other companies? These 3 valuation measures might help you decide.

Is Hillman Solutions Making Efficient Use Of Its Profits?

Hillman Solutions doesn't pay any regular dividends currently which essentially means that it has been reinvesting all of its profits into the business. This definitely contributes to the high earnings growth number that we discussed above.

Conclusion

Overall, we feel that Hillman Solutions certainly does have some positive factors to consider. With a high rate of reinvestment, albeit at a low ROE, the company has managed to see a considerable growth in its earnings. Having said that, the company's earnings growth is expected to slow down, as forecasted in the current analyst estimates. To know more about the latest analysts predictions for the company, check out this visualization of analyst forecasts for the company.

data.title: Epiroc (STO:EPI A) Has Affirmed Its Dividend Of SEK1.90
data.description:
The board of Epiroc AB (publ) (STO:EPI A) has announced that it will pay a dividend of SEK1.90 per share on the 12th of May. The dividend yield is 1.5% based on this payment, which is a little bit low compared to the other companies in the industry.

Epiroc's Payment Could Potentially Have Solid Earnings Coverage

It would be nice for the yield to be higher, but we should also check if higher levels of dividend payment would be sustainable. Prior to this announcement, Epiroc's dividend was comfortably covered by both cash flow and earnings. This indicates that a lot of the earnings are being reinvested into the business, with the aim of fueling growth.

Over the next year, EPS is forecast to expand by 36.5%. Assuming the dividend continues along recent trends, we think the payout ratio could be 43% by next year, which is in a pretty sustainable range.

View our latest analysis for Epiroc

Epiroc Doesn't Have A Long Payment History

Even though the company has been paying a consistent dividend for a while, we would like to see a few more years before we feel comfortable relying on it. Since 2019, the dividend has gone from SEK2.10 total annually to SEK3.80. This works out to be a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of approximately 8.8% a year over that time. Epiroc has a nice track record of dividend growth but we would wait until we see a longer track record before getting too confident.

The Dividend Has Growth Potential

The company's investors will be pleased to have been receiving dividend income for some time. It's encouraging to see that Epiroc has been growing its earnings per share at 9.7% a year over the past five years. Shareholders are getting plenty of the earnings returned to them, which combined with strong growth makes this quite appealing.

Epiroc Looks Like A Great Dividend Stock

Overall, we think that this is a great income investment, and we think that maintaining the dividend this year may have been a conservative choice. Earnings are easily covering distributions, and the company is generating plenty of cash. Taking this all into consideration, this looks like it could be a good dividend opportunity.

Investors generally tend to favour companies with a consistent, stable dividend policy as opposed to those operating an irregular one. However, there are other things to consider for investors when analysing stock performance. Companies that are growing earnings tend to be the best dividend stocks over the long term. data.title: Honda Prelude Return And AI Push Meet Fuel Cell And Governance Tests
data.description:
• Honda Motor (TSE:7267) is relaunching the Prelude coupe after roughly 25 years, signaling a fresh push in its product lineup.
  • The company is using Matlantis AI to support materials development in its R&D efforts.
  • Investors have raised concerns about Honda's sale of its stake in Yutaka Giken and broader governance practices.
  • Honda has ended its fuel cell joint venture with General Motors, marking a shift in its hydrogen technology approach.

Honda Motor (TSE:7267), trading at ¥1,529.5, is drawing attention as several company specific stories hit at once. The stock has returned 8.3% over the past year and 90.7% over five years, figures that keep Honda on the radar for investors tracking large global automakers.

For shareholders and potential investors, these developments relate to product direction, technology choices, and board level decision making. How Honda executes on the Prelude relaunch, applies AI in materials work, responds to governance criticism, and reshapes its fuel cell plans may influence how the market views risk and opportunity around TSE:7267 over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Honda Motor by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Honda Motor.

How Honda Motor stacks up against its biggest competitors

For you as an investor, this cluster of moves points to Honda refining where it wants to compete and how it spends its research budget. Relaunching the Prelude with a hybrid powertrain keeps Honda in the performance coupe conversation alongside Toyota, Nissan and other global automakers, while the adoption of Matlantis AI for materials work suggests a push to make R&D more efficient, especially for complex battery and hydrogen related components.

Advertisement

How this fits the Honda Motor narrative investors are debating

The Prelude relaunch and Matlantis AI adoption feed directly into the ongoing debate between bearish and bullish narratives around Honda's long term electrification and technology capabilities. At the same time, the open letter on the Yutaka Giken sale and questions about treasury shares speak to governance and capital allocation, which are central to how both narrative camps frame Honda's ability to balance product investment with shareholder returns.

Risks and rewards investors are weighing right now

  • ⚠️ Governance concerns over the Yutaka Giken stake sale and large treasury shareholding could pressure Honda to adjust capital allocation policies or board practices.
  • ⚠️ Ending the General Motors fuel cell joint venture concentrates execution risk in Honda's internal hydrogen program, especially if future partnerships are slower to materialize.
  • 🎁 Using Matlantis AI for faster materials screening may shorten development cycles for batteries and hydrogen systems, which could support cost and performance improvements over time.
  • 🎁 The Prelude relaunch, together with ongoing hybrid efforts, keeps Honda present in enthusiast and higher margin segments that investors often watch closely when comparing it with rivals like Toyota and Ford.

What to watch next

From here, it is worth tracking how quickly Honda converts the Prelude relaunch, AI assisted R&D and its solo hydrogen push into clear product and margin outcomes, while also seeing whether the board responds to shareholder pressure on the Yutaka Giken transaction and treasury shares. data.title: Is Century Communities (CCS) Pricing Reflect Housing Market And Rate Expectations Accurately
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Century Communities at around US$63.99 is offering good value today, it helps to step back and look at what the current price is really baking in.
  • The stock has been mixed recently, with a 7.4% decline over the last week, a 7.2% gain over the past month, an 8.6% return year to date, and a 12.6% decline over the last year, while the 3 year and 5 year returns sit at 5.6% and 41.1% respectively.
  • Recent news flow around Century Communities has largely focused on housing market conditions, interest rate expectations, and builder sentiment, all of which can influence how investors view homebuilder stocks. These themes help frame why the share price has seen both shorter term swings and longer term shifts in sentiment.
  • On our checklist based valuation framework, Century Communities scores 2 out of 6, which sets up a closer look at how different valuation methods treat the stock, and a later section that walks through an even more complete way to think about value.

Century Communities scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Century Communities Dividend Discount Model (DDM) Analysis

The Dividend Discount Model looks at a stock as the present value of all future dividends, adjusted for how fast those dividends are expected to grow and the return investors require.

For Century Communities, the model uses a current dividend per share of US$1.16 and a return on equity of 13.32%. The payout ratio used is 9.91%, which points to dividends being a relatively small portion of earnings, with most profits retained in the business. Simply Wall St caps the long run dividend growth rate at 3.26%, even though the underlying expected growth input is 12.00%, to avoid assuming very high growth far into the future.

Using these inputs in the DDM gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$18.71 per share. Compared with the current share price of around US$63.99, this suggests the stock is very expensive on this dividend based measure, with an intrinsic discount indicating it is 242.0% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Century Communities Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Century Communities, the P/E ratio is a useful yardstick because it tells you how much you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. In general, investors tend to accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how risky they think those earnings are.

Century Communities is trading on a P/E of 8.77x. That sits below the broader Consumer Durables industry average of 11.24x and slightly above the peer group average of 7.95x, so on simple comparisons the stock is somewhere in the middle of the pack. To go a step further, Simply Wall St also estimates a Fair Ratio of 10.97x for Century Communities.

The Fair Ratio is a proprietary P/E estimate that reflects the company’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics, rather than just lining it up against a blunt industry or peer average. Because it is tailored to the company’s own fundamentals, it can give you a more specific sense of whether the current P/E looks stretched or conservative. With the shares at 8.77x versus a Fair Ratio of 10.97x, the stock screens as undervalued on this earnings based yardstick.

data.title: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) Margin Compression And EPS Collapse Test Bullish Narratives
data.description:
UnitedHealth Group (UNH) closed FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$113.2b and basic EPS of US$0.01. This set a very different quarterly earnings profile against a full year in which trailing 12 month revenue reached US$447.6b and EPS came in at US$13.23. Over recent quarters, the company has seen revenue move from US$100.8b and EPS of US$5.98 in Q4 2024 to US$109.6b and US$6.90 in Q1 2025, US$111.6b and US$3.76 in Q2, US$113.2b and US$2.59 in Q3, before landing at US$113.2b and US$0.01 in Q4. This leaves investors to weigh solid top line scale against pressure on margins and profit conversion.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the widely followed stories around growth, profitability and risk to see which narratives still hold up and which ones the latest figures push back on.

Margins Tighten With 2.7% Net Profit Level

  • Over the last 12 months, UnitedHealth Group earned US$12.1b of net income on US$447.6b of revenue, which works out to a 2.7% net profit margin compared with 3.6% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that this lower 2.7% margin sits alongside past earnings being described as high quality and earnings growth of 0.5% per year over five years. This creates a contrast between:
    • Quarterly net income moving from US$6.3b in Q1 2025 to US$10 million in Q4 2025, showing how headline profit can swing even when full year revenue is large and steady.
    • Trailing 12 month EPS of US$13.23, which is well below the earlier US$24.07 at Q1 2025, so supporters of the bullish story need to judge whether these swings fit within their long term quality argument.

Valuation Tension, 22.1x P/E vs DCF Fair Value

  • UNH trades on a trailing P/E of 22.1x, above the 19.8x peer average and roughly in line with the US Healthcare industry at 22x, while the provided DCF fair value is US$795.27 versus a market price of US$294.02.
  • For bullish investors, this combination heavily supports the idea that the stock may be priced below intrinsic value. Yet it also raises questions that matter for more cautious readers:
    • The DCF fair value of US$795.27 is much higher than both the current US$294.02 share price and the US$373.60 analyst target, so the gap between these three reference points is wide.
    • Analysts cited in the input point to about 27.1% implied upside from US$294.02 to US$373.60, and that sits alongside a 3.01% dividend yield, which bullish holders may see as support while they weigh margin compression and debt levels.
On these numbers, many investors will want to understand why a 22.1x P/E and falling margin still come alongside such a large DCF gap and analyst upside signals. 📊 Read the full UnitedHealth Group Consensus Narrative.

Earnings Growth Forecasts vs Recent EPS Swings

  • Earnings are forecast to grow about 15.95% per year while revenue growth is forecast around 3.7% per year, set against trailing 12 month EPS of US$13.23 and quarterly EPS that ranged from US$6.90 in Q1 2025 to US$0.01 in Q4 2025.
  • What is striking for anyone weighing a more cautious angle is how these growth forecasts interact with recent swings in reported profit:
    • Quarterly net income went from US$6.3b in Q1 2025 to US$2.3b in Q3 and then US$10 million in Q4, which is a much sharper move than the smoother revenue path from US$109.6b to US$113.2b over the same period.
    • At the same time, the 3.7% revenue growth forecast and 15.95% earnings growth forecast sit against a lower trailing margin and a high level of debt flagged as a risk, so investors may focus on how comfortably future earnings can cover that balance sheet.

data.title: Greif (GEF) EPS Surge To US$3.76 Tests Skeptical One Off Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Greif (GEF) opened Q1 2026 with revenue of US$994.8 million and basic EPS of US$3.76, setting a clear reference point against last year’s results. The company has seen revenue move from US$1.02 billion in Q1 2025 to US$994.8 million in Q1 2026, while basic EPS shifted from US$0.14 to US$3.76 over the same period. This came alongside a trailing net profit margin of 4.4% compared with 4.3% a year earlier. That mix of higher profitability on a relatively steady revenue base puts the focus squarely on how durable these margins look from here.

See our full analysis for Greif.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how they line up with the widely followed narratives around Greif’s earnings power, growth outlook, and risk profile.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

US$176.6m net income stands out

  • Net income excluding extra items came in at US$176.6m for Q1 2026 on US$994.8m of revenue, versus US$6.6m on US$1.02b of revenue in Q1 2025, so profit levels look very different even though the top line is in a similar ballpark.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that this profit step up sits next to a 4.4% net margin over the last year, only slightly above 4.3% previously, which means:
    • Optimistic investors pointing to forecast earnings growth of about 15.7% a year can say recent profitability is already supporting that story, at least on the period’s numbers.
    • At the same time, the fairly small shift in trailing margin shows most of the improvement is not coming from a big structural jump in profitability, so the bullish case still leans heavily on the future growth forecasts rather than a long history of high margins.
To see how this profit profile lines up with longer term growth hopes, check out how the full narrative treats this latest jump in earnings. 📊 Read the full Greif Consensus Narrative.

4.4% margin and a US$93.0m one off

  • Over the last 12 months, Greif reported a 4.4% net profit margin and the analysis points to a US$93.0m one off gain that had a material impact on those results, so the trailing profitability number is not purely from repeatable operations.
  • Skeptical investors often worry that one off items inflate quality, and the data here gives them material backing because:
    • The US$93.0m gain sits inside a period where net income excluding extra items is US$186.5m on about US$4.3b of revenue, so a meaningful slice of the bottom line over the year ties back to that single non recurring item.
    • When bears combine this with weak free cash flow coverage of the 3.09% dividend and reported insider selling over the past three months, they can argue that current payout and profit levels are less robust than the headline margin might suggest.

P/E of 18.4x and DCF fair value gap

  • Greif is trading on a P/E of 18.4x, below the 21.1x average for the North American Packaging industry, and the provided DCF fair value is US$255.16 per share versus the current price of US$72.46, indicating the stock has been around 71.6% below that modelled value.
  • Supporters of the bullish valuation story lean on this gap, but the figures also build in some checks and balances because:
    • The lower P/E than the industry fits with the idea of potential upside, yet the ratio is still above the peer average in the data, so the stock is not uniformly cheap across every comparison set.
    • When you overlay the forecast earnings growth of roughly 15.7% a year on that valuation picture, the bullish case argues the current P/E and DCF fair value leave plenty of room, while more cautious investors will point back to the role of the US$93.0m one off and modest 1.9% expected revenue growth when judging how much weight to put on the DCF output.
